extra.message:
JWT Signature Verification Disabled

The JWT decode function explicitly disables signature verification (verify_signature=False) and expiration checks (verify_exp=False). This allows anyone to forge valid JWT tokens by modifying the payload and removing the signature, completely bypassing authentication. Combined with the hardcoded secret, this is a critical authentication bypass vulnerability.

Fix: Remove the verify_signature and verify_exp options to enable default verification. Use the flask_graphql_auth library's built-in token verification methods instead of manual JWT decoding.

Path Traversal in File Upload

The save_file function concatenates user-controlled filename directly to the upload directory path without sanitization. Attackers can use path traversal sequences (../../../) to write files to arbitrary locations on the filesystem, potentially overwriting system files or writing to web directories for code execution.

Fix: Validate filenames against a strict allowlist pattern (e.g., alphanumeric only), use os.path.basename() to strip directory components, and verify the resolved path is within the intended upload directory using os.path.commonpath() or similar checks.

OS Command Injection in ImportPaste Mutation

The ImportPaste GraphQL mutation constructs a shell command using user-controlled URL parameters (scheme, host, port, path) and executes it via helpers.run_cmd(). The strip_dangerous_characters() function only removes semicolons and ampersands in hard mode, leaving pipe operators, backticks, command substitution $(), and other shell metacharacters exploitable for arbitrary command execution.

Fix: Never execute shell commands with user-controlled input. Use HTTP client libraries (requests, httpx) directly instead of shelling out to curl. If shell execution is absolutely necessary, use subprocess with shell=False and a strict allowlist of characters.

OS Command Injection in ImportPaste Mutation

The ImportPaste GraphQL mutation constructs a shell command using user-controlled URL parameters (scheme, host, port, path) and executes it via helpers.run_cmd(). The strip_dangerous_characters() function only removes semicolons and ampersands in hard mode, leaving pipe operators, backticks, command substitution $(), and other shell metacharacters exploitable for arbitrary command execution.

Fix: Never execute shell commands with user-controlled input. Use HTTP client libraries (requests, httpx) directly instead of shelling out to curl. If shell execution is absolutely necessary, use subprocess with shell=False and a strict allowlist of characters.

OS Command Injection in ImportPaste Mutation

The ImportPaste GraphQL mutation constructs a shell command using user-controlled URL parameters (scheme, host, port, path) and executes it via helpers.run_cmd(). The strip_dangerous_characters() function only removes semicolons and ampersands in hard mode, leaving pipe operators, backticks, command substitution $(), and other shell metacharacters exploitable for arbitrary command execution.

Fix: Never execute shell commands with user-controlled input. Use HTTP client libraries (requests, httpx) directly instead of shelling out to curl. If shell execution is absolutely necessary, use subprocess with shell=False and a strict allowlist of characters.

SQL Injection in resolve_pastes

The resolve_pastes resolver uses Python string formatting (% operator) inside SQLAlchemy's text() construct to build a SQL WHERE clause with the user-controlled 'filter' parameter. This allows attackers to inject arbitrary SQL syntax, enabling data extraction, schema enumeration, or data modification.

Fix: Use SQLAlchemy's parameterized queries with literal binds or ORM filtering: result.filter((Paste.title == filter) | (Paste.content == filter)). Never use Python string formatting or concatenation in SQL queries.

DOM-Based XSS via jQuery Template Literals in Paste Display The JavaScript constructs HTML content using ES6 template literals with data from GraphQL/WebSocket responses and inserts it into the DOM using jQuery's .append() method. Since jQuery parses HTML strings passed to append(), malicious JavaScript payloads stored in paste titles, content, owner names, or user agents will execute immediately. For example, a paste with title '' will steal cookies when rendered. Fix: Use textContent instead of HTML insertion, or sanitize data using DOMPurify or similar library before DOM insertion. Escape HTML entities in JavaScript: title.replace(//g, '>')

DOM-Based XSS via jQuery Template Literals in Paste Display The JavaScript constructs HTML content using ES6 template literals with data from GraphQL/WebSocket responses and inserts it into the DOM using jQuery's .append() method. Since jQuery parses HTML strings passed to append(), malicious JavaScript payloads stored in paste titles, content, owner names, or user agents will execute immediately. For example, a paste with title '' will steal cookies when rendered. Fix: Use textContent instead of HTML insertion, or sanitize data using DOMPurify or similar library before DOM insertion. Escape HTML entities in JavaScript: title.replace(//g, '>')

Hardcoded Flask SECRET_KEY

The Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded to the static value 'dvga'. This key is used for session management and cryptographic operations. An attacker with access to this key can forge session cookies, bypass authentication, and perform CSRF attacks.

Impact: Attackers can forge session cookies to impersonate users or administrators, bypassing authentication controls.

Fix: Load the SECRET_KEY from environment variables or a secure secrets management system (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager). Ensure the key is cryptographically random and at least 32 bytes in length.

Plaintext Password Storage/Comparison

The Login mutation queries the database comparing plaintext passwords directly: User.query.filter_by(username=username, password=password).first(). This indicates passwords are stored in plaintext without hashing, and compared without proper authentication mechanisms.

Impact: Attackers who gain database access obtain plaintext passwords. Users with reused passwords across services are compromised. Violates security best practices and compliance requirements.

Fix: Implement proper password hashing using bcrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2. Use constant-time comparison functions to prevent timing attacks.

Denial of Service via simulate_load

The resolve_system_update resolver calls security.simulate_load() which contains an unbounded while loop with sleep(0.1) that iterates up to a random limit (200-500 iterations). This consumes server resources and can be exploited to cause DoS by repeatedly calling this query.

Impact: Server resource exhaustion causing unavailability for legitimate users.

Fix: Remove debug/test functionality from production code. If load testing endpoints are needed, require authentication and rate limiting. Implement query cost analysis properly to reject expensive queries.

Access Control Bypass via X-DVGA-MODE Header

The set_difficulty before_request handler allows unauthenticated users to change the application security mode from 'hard' to 'easy' by sending the X-DVGA-MODE header. In 'easy' mode, all security middleware (depth limiting, cost analysis, introspection blocking, denylist checks) is bypassed, effectively disabling all protections.

Fix: Require authentication and authorization (admin role) to change security modes. Alternatively, only allow mode changes through configuration files or environment variables, not via HTTP headers.

Excessive Data Exposure - Password Field in UserObject

The UserObject GraphQL type includes a resolve_password method that returns the actual password hash/value when the context identity is 'admin'. This exposes sensitive credential information through the API.

Impact: Administrators or attackers who can set identity context to 'admin' can retrieve all user passwords/hashes, facilitating credential theft and lateral movement.

Fix: Remove the password field from the GraphQL schema entirely. Never expose password hashes or authentication credentials through API responses.

Sensitive Information Exposure in Audit Logs

The audit log template renders raw GraphQL queries (`{{i.gqlquery}}`) in an HTML table without redacting sensitive information. If users submit mutations containing passwords, authentication tokens, or personally identifiable information in query variables, this data is permanently stored and displayed in the audit interface accessible to administrators.

Impact: Administrators or attackers with access to the audit page can view sensitive GraphQL mutations containing user passwords, JWT tokens, or private data. This creates a significant data exposure risk violating privacy requirements and security best practices.

Fix: Implement log sanitization to redact sensitive fields (passwords, tokens, PII) before storing queries in the audit log. Alternatively, use parameterized logging that separates query structure from variable values and never logs sensitive variables.

Weak Input Sanitization

The strip_dangerous_characters function attempts to sanitize input for command injection but only removes semicolons and ampersands. This is insufficient as it leaves pipe operators (|), backticks (`), dollar parentheses ($()), redirection operators (>, <), and other shell metacharacters that can be used for command injection.

Fix: Do not rely on blacklists or partial sanitization for security. Use parameterized approaches (subprocess with shell=False) or strict allowlists of allowed characters (alphanumeric only) rather than attempting to remove dangerous characters.

Timing Attack in Credential Verification

The check_creds function returns immediately if the username is not 'admin', revealing whether a username exists via timing analysis. This allows attackers to enumerate valid usernames. Additionally, the password comparison uses == which may be vulnerable to timing attacks depending on the Python implementation.

Fix: Use constant-time comparison for both username and password verification. Return a generic error message ('Invalid credentials') regardless of whether the username or password was incorrect to prevent user enumeration.

Denial of Service via simulate_load

The simulate_load function performs a resource-intensive operation with random iteration counts (200-500) in a tight loop with sleep delays. The resolve_system_update resolver exposes this as a GraphQL query, allowing attackers to exhaust server resources.

Fix: Remove intentional resource exhaustion functions from production code. Implement rate limiting and query cost analysis to prevent DoS attacks.

Information Disclosure via Detailed Error Messages

The format_custom_error function exposes internal implementation details including stack traces, file system paths, exception messages, and debug information to API consumers. This aids attackers in reconnaissance and vulnerability discovery.

Impact: Information leakage aids attackers in understanding application internals, file structure, and potential vulnerabilities. Stack traces may reveal sensitive data or authentication credentials.

Fix: Implement generic error messages for production environments. Log detailed errors server-side only. Use a whitelist approach to expose only safe, non-sensitive error information to clients.

GraphQL Batch Query Abuse

The GraphQL view has batch=True enabled, allowing clients to send multiple queries in a single HTTP request (array of queries). This can be used to bypass rate limiting, execute mutation batches for brute force attacks, or cause denial of service through resource exhaustion.

Fix: Disable batch processing (batch=False) or implement strict cost analysis and rate limiting on batched queries. Limit the number of queries per batch (e.g., maximum 5).

Missing Critical Security Headers

The application base templates lack essential security headers including Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, X-XSS-Protection, and Referrer-Policy. This leaves the application vulnerable to clickjacking, MIME-sniffing attacks, and XSS.

Impact: Missing headers enable clickjacking attacks (embedding the app in malicious iframes), MIME-type sniffing leading to XSS, and reduce overall browser-side security protections.

Fix: Add security headers via Flask-Talisman or web server configuration: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-XSS-Protection: 1; mode=block.

Missing Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) Protection

All HTML forms (Create Paste, Import Paste, Upload Paste) lack CSRF tokens. While the main application uses GraphQL (which typically uses JSON content-type and is less vulnerable to CSRF), these specific forms submit to server-side endpoints and are vulnerable to CSRF attacks. An attacker can trick authenticated users into performing unwanted actions (creating pastes, importing malicious content) via malicious websites.

Fix: Implement Flask-WTF or similar CSRF protection middleware. Add CSRF tokens to all state-changing forms and validate them on the server side for every POST/PUT/DELETE request.

Information Disclosure via Solution Documentation

The solution templates document detailed exploitation techniques for vulnerabilities including SQL injection (solution_15.html), OS command injection (solution_10.html, solution_11.html), path traversal (solution_18.html), and weak passwords (solution_19.html). While intended for educational purposes, this provides attackers with exact exploitation steps and confirms vulnerability existence.

Impact: Attackers can use the built-in solutions as a roadmap to exploit the application, confirming the exact nature of vulnerabilities, payload formats, and exploitation techniques without requiring reconnaissance.

Fix: If this is a production training environment, restrict solution access to authenticated administrators only. Consider removing solution files from production deployments or implementing strict access controls.

Insecure Cookie Configuration for 'env' Cookie

The application uses an 'env' cookie to control access to the GraphiQL interface (graphiql:disable/enable). Based on the application context and solution documentation, this cookie is set without HttpOnly, Secure, or SameSite flags, making it vulnerable to theft and manipulation.

Impact: The 'env' cookie can be stolen via XSS (lacking HttpOnly) or intercepted over unencrypted connections (lacking Secure flag). Attackers can manipulate this cookie to enable the GraphiQL interface in production, facilitating further attacks.

Fix: Set the 'env' cookie with HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite=Strict flags. Consider using secure server-side session storage instead of client-side cookies for security-sensitive settings.

Missing Comma in allowed_cmds Tuple

There is a missing comma between 'ps' and 'whoami' in the allowed_cmds tuple, creating the string 'pswhoami' instead of two separate strings. This weakens the intended security control by not properly restricting the 'ps' command.

Fix: Add the missing comma: ('echo', 'ps', 'whoami', 'tail'). However, relying on startswith for command validation is still insecure and should be replaced with a strict allowlist of exact command matches.

Insecure TLS Configuration in curl

The ImportPaste mutation uses 'curl --insecure' which disables SSL/TLS certificate validation. This makes the application vulnerable to Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks when fetching external URLs, as malicious certificates will be accepted.

Fix: Remove the --insecure flag and ensure proper certificate validation is performed when making HTTPS requests. Use a proper HTTP client library that respects system certificate stores.

Potential Log Injection via Operation Names

The audit log displays GraphQL operation names (gqloperation) without sanitization. While Jinja2 auto-escapes HTML, if these logs are also written to plaintext log files or if the | safe filter is mistakenly applied elsewhere, attackers could inject newline characters or control sequences into operation names to spoof log entries or corrupt log file formatting.

Fix: Sanitize operation names to allow only alphanumeric characters and underscores. Reject queries with operation names containing special characters, whitespace, or control sequences before logging.
